trajectory_summary: The call moves from an engaged, curious customer to a booked appointment, but the path is reactive throughout. Joel follows Eva's question agenda rather than running his own call structure, resulting in two rounds of scope creep before he finds the scope-hold move. The bill anchor is captured but never flipped. The button-up is incomplete — Joel closes with the wrong day ('next Wednesday' at 11:18 when the appointment is Tuesday the 28th), creating an immediate no-show risk.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment (Tuesday the 28th at 7PM), so a 'booked' outcome tag would be technically correct. However, the appointment is structurally fragile: Joel confirms the wrong day at close ('we'll see you next Wednesday' when the date is a Tuesday), the button-up is missing the one-hour block, the no-driving/at-computer anchor, and the 'is that fair' verbal handshake. The sit is at elevated no-show risk.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: thank you for your time today, and we'll see you next Wednesday.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The appointment is Tuesday the 28th. Joel closes with 'next Wednesday.' Eva does not correct him. This is an active no-show risk that requires an immediate check on whether the follow-up text stated the correct day. In the 1-on-1, this becomes the anchor for the full button-up protocol: correct day/date/time read-back, one-hour block, no-driving/at-computer anchor, 'is that fair' verbal handshake. Joel completed only the text-followup promise from the required button-up elements.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. How much does it cost?
key_moments.stewart_read: Eva's opening cost question is the textbook trigger for the objection_inversion move: 'What you can't afford is your current bill.' Joel instead delivers a pitch line about no upfront cost. The flip was available, grounded in Eva's own stated bill pain ('really high PGE bills' at 01:36 — though that comes slightly later, the pattern is the same). Strategic read: Joel may have been warming Eva before deploying the flip. Reactive read: Joel defaulted to the pitch script and the flip never came across the entire call. The reactive read holds — the flip is never deployed at any of Eva's three cost inquiries.

key_moments.quote: and a credit score of six fifty or higher. Would that be the case for you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Two issues compound here. First, Joel states the threshold as 650, not 670 — consistent with the drift flagged in SESSION18_fcbf33c1. Second, and more critically, Joel bundles taxable income AND credit score into a single compound question. Eva answers 'Yes' to both with one word at 02:42. Her credit score is never individually confirmed. Per the Spencer operator note in the schema, stated credit is unreliable (Vantage vs FICO, bureau variance). A compound 'yes' to a bundled qualifier is the weakest possible confirmation.

key_moments.quote: And what's the cost of repair? Like, if something needs to be repaired out, is that cost?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the one moment Joel correctly holds scope. His response — 'I'm not too sure about that part, just because I'm in scheduling... I would note that that's something you're curious about... we could have a specialist share that with you' — is the right move. The framing is slightly self-deprecating ('I don't know too much') but the bridge to the specialist is clean and the note-taking gesture ('I would note that') is a micro-trust move. Contrast this with his full technical answers at 00:25 and 03:46 — Joel has the scope-hold available but only deploys it when he genuinely doesn't know the answer.

key_moments.quote: What about in the different seasons, like, when it get cold in the fall and the winter? Do you cover it up, or how does that work?
key_moments.stewart_read: Joel's response is a full technical explanation of panel absorption, radiation penetration, net metering, and seasonal energy banking — approximately 30 seconds of closer-tier content. This is the deepest scope-creep moment on the call. The explanation is accurate but unnecessary: Eva is already engaged and moving toward a booking. Each technical answer risks generating a new question (which it does — the repair-cost question follows immediately). Strategic read: Joel may have been building confidence in the product to smooth the close. Reactive read: Joel defaulted to answering because he knew the answer. The reactive read holds — the scope-hold move was available and unused.
